samples.label: Hex · coded
samples.caption: 203 coded 16-sector rings on a hex lattice — the classic ringgrid board. Every ring decodes to a stable ID against the 893-word codebook, so the RANSAC homography anchors an absolute board frame with no origin dots.

samples.label: Rect · coded
samples.caption: 400 coded rings on a 20×20 square lattice. The coded detection path is lattice-agnostic, so decoded IDs anchor an absolute board frame on a rect grid exactly as on hex — again without origin dots.

samples.label: Hex · plain + dots
samples.caption: 203 plain rings on a hex lattice with an auto-placed asymmetric origin-dot triad (violet). Plain rings carry no ID, so markers are labeled by lattice coordinate; the dots break every lattice rotation to resolve an absolute board frame and orientation.

samples.label: Rect · plain + dots
samples.caption: 576 plain rings on a 24×24 square lattice anchored by a three-dot origin triad (violet) auto-placed in the center gaps. The dots recover an absolute board frame from an otherwise symmetric grid.

samples.label: Hex · plain, no dots
samples.caption: 203 plain rings on a hex lattice with no origin dots. Without IDs or dots the board is labeled only up to its lattice symmetry (a relative frame); recovering the complete board is the success criterion — board_complete is true here.

samples.label: Rect · plain, no dots
samples.caption: 576 plain rings on a 24×24 square lattice, no origin dots. Labeled up to the lattice symmetry (relative frame); success means the whole board was recovered — board_complete is true here.
